sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 30, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now the black L-shape is at cols 51-59, which is exactly where the yellow L-shape was. The yellow L-shape has been mostly consumed/covered, but some yellow pixels remain at specific positions:
- (46, 52), (46, 55), (46, 58) - top row of the L-shape
- A 18-pixel yellow piece at [[48, 51], [48, 53], [53, 53], [53, 51]] - this is the vertical bar on the left side of the L-shape
- White dots at (49, 58) and (52, 58) - the vertical bar of the white dot pattern
The black L-shape boundary is [[45, 51], [45, 59], [53, 59], [53, 57], [48, 57], [47, 56], [47, 51]] which matches the original yellow L-shape's boundary shape.
Looking at the image, I can see the black L-shape now occupies the yellow L-shape's position. The yellow pixels that remain are inside the black L-shape's interior (the hollow part). The white dots are also inside the black L-shape.
It seems like the black L-shape is a "container" and the yellow L-shape is the "target" that gets revealed inside. The goal might be to position the black L-shape so the white dots align with specific parts of the yellow L-shape.
Looking at the current state:
- Yellow pixels at (46, 52), (46, 55), (46, 58) - these are on the top horizontal bar
- Yellow piece at rows 48-53, cols 51-53 - this is the left vertical bar of the L
- White dots at (49, 58), (52, 58) - these are on the right vertical bar
The white dots seem to be positioned on the right side of the L-shape, while the yellow pixels are on the left side and top.
Maybe the goal is to have the white dots match up with the yellow pixels. Currently they're on opposite sides. Let me think about what configuration would be "correct".
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the game. The black L-shape seems to be a "frame" or "window" that reveals what's underneath. The yellow L-shape is underneath. The white dots are markers inside the black frame.
Maybe the goal is to position the black frame so the white dots align with the yellow L-shape's pixels. Currently:
- White dots at (49, 58) and (52, 58) are on the right edge of the L-shape
- Yellow pixels at (46, 52), (46, 55), (46, 58) are on the top bar
- Yellow piece at (48-53, 51-53) is the left vertical bar
The white dots don't overlap with yellow pixels. Maybe I need to shift the black L-shape so the white dots land on yellow pixels.
Let me try moving LEFT to shift the black frame leftward, which would move the white dots leftward relative to the yellow shape.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me check the ascii to see the exact layout of the overlapping region
lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Overlap region (rows 45-53, cols 48-60):")
for r in range(45, 54):
    print(f"{r}: {lines[r][48:61]}")
print()
# The black L-shape boundary is [[45, 51], [45, 59], [53, 59], [53, 57], [48, 57], [47, 56], [47, 51]]
# Yellow pixels: (46,52), (46,55), (46,58), plus vertical bar at rows 48-53, cols 51-53
# White dots: (49,58), (52,58)
# Try LEFT to shift black frame left, moving white dots toward yellow pixels
action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("After LEFT:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 70:
        print(f"  id={n['id']} color={n['color']} pixels={n['pixels']} boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
